detail.note: 890 words / 200 wpm = 4.45 min; sidecar reports 5 min. Both within [4, 6].
References to decomposition and abstraction (lines 93–94) are backward references to prior topics 1.5 and 1.6 — not forward references. Verified against corpus prerequisites section.

sample.assertion: A problem statement is a short, focused piece of writing — exactly three sentences — that pins down the problem before any solution thinking begins
A problem domain is the specific area of real-world activity your project addresses. It is not a technology or a tool — it is a slice of real life.
Examples of domains: healthcare, student revision, local transport, food waste, personal finance
A problem statement is a short written description of who is affected, what specifically goes wrong, and why it matters. It is not a solution proposal.
Three rules: Specific not general, One problem not a list, No solution embedded
3-sentence table: Sentence 1 Context, Sentence 2 Specific problem, Sentence 3 Impact
Worked example — student revision domain (secondary school students / over-revise familiar / needs to track understanding)
Counter-example: 'Healthcare has a lot of problems. Technology could help make things better for everyone.'
Five-step checklist: Choose domain, Name who is affected, State what goes wrong, State consequence, Write and test
Problem domain = real-world area; problem statement = three sentences covering context, specific problem, and impact
